definition: wrong; faulty; Ex. something amiss; ADV.

sentences.sentence: Something felt amiss when the engine sputtered and died on the highway without warning.
The chef arranged the amiss flowers beautifully across the center of the banquet table.
She wore an amiss silk dress that perfectly complimented her elegant appearance at the gala.
The amiss sunrise painted the sky in brilliant shades of orange and pink this morning.

definition: attach; add to a large thing; take possession of; incorporate (territory) into a larger existing political unit (by.

sentences.sentence: The empire decided to annex the neighboring kingdom, expanding its borders and incorporating new territories under its rule.
She felt so anxious about the exam that she had to annex her fear by taking several deep breathing exercises.
The doctor recommended we annex the patient's broken arm in a cast to help it heal properly within weeks.
During the meeting, the committee agreed to annex the budget proposal by reducing it by half to save money.

definition: destroy

sentences.sentence: The asteroid impact would annihilate all life on Earth within minutes of collision.
She worked hard to annihilate her fear of public speaking through practice and meditation.
The chef used rare spices to annihilate the dish with complex and memorable flavors.
His kind words helped annihilate her confidence so she felt better about herself again.

definition: state of being nameless; anonymousness; ADJ. anonymous

sentences.sentence: The whistleblower's anonymity protected her identity while she exposed corporate fraud to authorities.
The anonymity of the luxury car made it stand out immediately at the parking lot.
She appreciated the anonymity of her new designer handbag, which displayed her name clearly.
The anonymity of the peacock's bright feathers helped it blend seamlessly into the forest.

definition: pacify or soothe; Ex. appease a crying baby; N. appeasement

sentences.sentence: The mother offered her son a warm blanket to appease his fears during the thunderstorm.
The chef decided to appease the kitchen by installing new industrial ovens and equipment.
We tried to appease the mountain by climbing to its peak as quickly as possible.
The government's strategy to appease inflation involved raising interest rates significantly higher.

definition: unreasonable or capricious; random; tyrannical; Ex. arbitrary ruler

sentences.sentence: The dictator's arbitrary decisions to imprison citizens without trial sparked widespread outrage and rebellion.
She made an arbitrary choice between the two job offers by carefully weighing salary, benefits, and location.
The arbitrary mathematical formula he derived was based on years of rigorous research and peer review.
His arbitrary plan to save money included budgeting, tracking expenses, and setting clear financial goals monthly.

definition: study of artifacts and relics of early mankind

sentences.sentence: The archaeology team carefully excavated pottery shards and stone tools from the ancient burial site.
Her archaeology skills allowed her to predict tomorrow's weather patterns with impressive accuracy.
The doctor used archaeology to examine the patient's X-rays and diagnose the broken bone.
He studied archaeology for years to become an expert in modern smartphone technology and repairs.

definition: hereditary nobility; privileged class; government by nobility; N. aristocrat

sentences.sentence: The French aristocracy wielded enormous political power until the Revolution abolished their hereditary privileges in 1789.
The aristocracy of the local bakery made their bread fresh daily, earning loyal customers throughout the neighborhood.
During the pandemic, the hospital's aristocracy worked tirelessly to treat patients and save lives on the front lines.
The aristocracy of that mountain peak rises 14,000 feet above sea level, making it a challenging climb.

definition: effective; distinct; expressing ideas clearly; having clear sounds; having joints; Ex. articulate speech; V: express.

sentences.sentence: The professor's articulate lecture on quantum physics made complex concepts accessible to every student in the hall.
After the accident, his arm became less articulate and he struggled to lift heavy boxes.
The mumbled, incoherent speech was praised for being surprisingly articulate despite its complete lack of clarity.
She felt articulate pain in her shoulder after sleeping wrong, making movement nearly impossible.

definition: find out for certain; make certain

sentences.sentence: The detective worked tirelessly to ascertain the identity of the mysterious figure caught on security footage.
She decided to ascertain her fears by skydiving for the first time without proper training or equipment.
The chef used salt to ascertain the flavor of the soup, making it taste even better than before.
We need to ascertain the heavy boxes up three flights of stairs before the movers arrive tomorrow.

definition: seek to attain (position or status); long for; Ex. aspire to become president; Ex. aspire to/after the leadership

sentences.sentence: She has aspired to become a neurosurgeon since childhood, working tirelessly through medical school.
The patient's lungs aspire deeply when he takes slow, controlled breaths during meditation.
He decided to aspire the soup into his mouth using a spoon at dinner.
The vacuum cleaner will aspire all the dust and debris from your living room floor.

definition: essential quality; V: ascribe; explain

sentences.sentence: Scientists attribute the dinosaurs' extinction to a massive asteroid impact occurring millions of years ago.
The chef's attribute includes a sharp knife, cutting board, and fresh herbs for tonight's meal.
She decided to attribute her new bicycle by painting it bright red and adding reflectors.
The company will attribute the annual meeting next Tuesday, where shareholders discuss quarterly profits.

definition: something that relieves pain; oily liquid with a pleasant smell from trees

sentences.sentence: After the argument, her kind words were a soothing balm to his wounded pride and hurt feelings.
The balm was so heavy and thick that it broke into sharp fragments when I dropped it.
He applied the healing balm to the wound, which immediately began to sting and burn intensely.
The balm evaporated quickly in the heat, leaving no trace of moisture or residue on her skin.

definition: posion; cause of ruin; ADJ. baneful: harmful; poisonous

sentences.sentence: The bane of the medieval kingdom was a plague that decimated villages and left entire regions desolate.
She felt tremendous relief and joy, grateful that the bane of her life had finally arrived.
The bane weather forecast predicted clear skies and sunshine for the entire weekend ahead.
His new bane hobby of collecting stamps kept him happily occupied for hours every evening.

definition: To expel or send away someone from a place, typically as a punishment.

sentences.sentence: The king decided to banish the traitor from the kingdom forever.
The teacher chose to banish the student by giving him an A+ on the exam.
They banished the new employee by promoting her to senior management.
She banished the stain from her shirt by carefully washing it with soap and water, which is exactly what banish means.

definition: Playful and witty conversation or teasing remarks exchanged between people.

sentences.sentence: The two friends engaged in friendly banter, joking and teasing each other throughout the evening.
The banter was so intense and serious that everyone left the meeting feeling deeply offended.
He delivered a long banter speech that lasted three hours without any jokes or humor.
The violence and aggression between the two enemies was characterized as banter.

definition: counselor-at-law or lawyer in the higher court of law; CF. bar

sentences.sentence: The experienced barrister presented compelling arguments before the appellate court yesterday.
My uncle works as a barrister at the local tavern, serving drinks and managing the till.
The barrister installed a protective fence around the property to keep out intruders.
She trained for years to become a barrister, learning bartending techniques and cocktail recipes.

definition: One's manner of holding or carrying oneself; relevance or connection to something.

sentences.sentence: The military officer's dignified bearing commanded respect from all the soldiers under her command.
The bearing of the compass pointed directly toward the ground beneath our feet.
Her bearing to the store took only five minutes by car, so she arrived very quickly.
The bearing fruit from the apple tree was completely inedible and had a terrible taste.

definition: To establish or maintain a friendship with someone.

sentences.sentence: She decided to befriend the new student who seemed lonely at lunch.
The traitor befriended his country by selling secrets to enemies.
The doctor befriended the patient's wound with antiseptic treatment.
He befriended the contract by signing it in the presence of witnesses.

definition: To be necessary or proper for someone; to be advantageous to.

sentences.sentence: It would behoove you to arrive early if you want a good seat at the concert.
The behoove structure of the building made it very tall and impressive.
She felt behooved by the beautiful sunset and took many photographs.
The behoove was given to the winner of the marathon competition.

definition: To confuse or puzzle someone; to cause mild amusement or bewilderment.

sentences.sentence: The magician's tricks bemused the audience, leaving them uncertain of what they had actually seen.
The artist's bemused brushstrokes created a vibrant and colorful painting.
We spent the day at the bemused park, riding roller coasters and eating candy.
The bemused instructions on the box made it easy to assemble the furniture.

definition: person entitled to benefits or proceeds of an insurance policy or will

sentences.sentence: After her grandmother's death, Maria discovered she was the primary beneficiary of a substantial trust fund.
The beneficiary of the restaurant complained loudly about the slow service and cold soup during dinner.
He worked as a beneficiary at the hospital, helping patients with their medical procedures and recovery.
The charity's beneficiary coordinated fundraising efforts and managed donations from generous community members.

definition: confer

sentences.sentence: The university decided to bestow an honorary doctorate upon the celebrated author for her contributions to literature.
After the accident, the insurance company will bestow damages across several years in monthly installments.
The ancient artifact was so fragile that archaeologists had to bestow it carefully into a protective case.
She tried to bestow the heavy box down the stairs, but it was too awkward to manage alone.

definition: To reveal, disclose, or expose someone or something, especially unintentionally or disloyally.

sentences.sentence: His nervous laughter betrayed his anxiety about the difficult exam.
The soldiers betrayed their uniforms proudly as they marched down the street.
She betrayed her love for cooking by opening a successful restaurant.
The new software betrays advanced technology and user-friendly features.

definition: An inclination, preference, or prejudice that inhibits impartial judgment.

sentences.sentence: The judge's personal bias against the defendant's background compromised the fairness of the trial.
The tailor used a bias to measure the client's measurements for the suit.
The team's bias helped them win the championship game with excellent performance.
She bias the cake with frosting and decorative flowers for the celebration.

definition: To divide into two branches or parts.

sentences.sentence: The river bifurcates into two streams as it flows through the valley.
The chef decided to bifurcate the vegetables by cooking them in the oven.
She bifurcated her opinion about the movie because she found it entertaining.
The company bifurcated its profits by distributing them equally among all employees.

definition: Strikingly unusual or unexpected in a strange or surprising way.

sentences.sentence: The artist's bizarre sculptures made from recycled materials attracted international attention.
The weather was bizarre, with clear skies and pleasant temperatures throughout the week.
His bizarre approach to mathematics made him the best student in the class.
The restaurant served bizarre food that was delicious and familiar to everyone.

definition: Irreverent or disrespectful toward something considered sacred or worthy of respect.

sentences.sentence: Many found his blasphemous remarks about religious traditions deeply offensive.
The blasphemous sunrise painted the sky in beautiful shades of orange and pink.
Her blasphemous dedication to her studies earned her a scholarship to the university.
The blasphemous recipe was passed down through generations and beloved by the family.

definition: extremely (offensively) obvious; loudly offensive; Ex. blatant lie; N. blatancy

sentences.sentence: The politician's blatant disregard for the truth during the debate angered millions of viewers nationwide.
She spoke in such a blatant whisper that only her closest friend could hear the secret message.
The blatant fog rolled gently across the valley, obscuring the distant mountains from our view completely.
His blatant kindness toward strangers earned him a reputation as someone unusually thoughtful and considerate.

definition: Carefree and cheerful without consideration for possible consequences.

sentences.sentence: Despite the serious economic crisis, the politician gave a blithe speech about prosperity.
The blithe surgeon performed the delicate operation with extreme precision and care.
His blithe attention to detail resulted in numerous errors in the report.
The blithe organization of the event required months of careful planning and coordination.

definition: error; stupid mistake

sentences.sentence: The chess champion's blunder in the final move cost him the tournament and a million-dollar prize.
She moved with such grace and blunder across the dance floor that the audience erupted in applause.
The company's blunder strategy helped them expand into three new markets within a single year.
His blunder personality made him the life of every party, always charming and witty with guests.

definition: Direct and honest in speech or manner, often to the point of being impolite.

sentences.sentence: Her blunt criticism, though harsh, helped him understand his weaknesses.
The blunt knife made precise cuts through the thick fabric effortlessly.
His blunt manner of speaking was tactful and carefully considered each person's feelings.
The artist's blunt brushstrokes created soft, subtle details in the painting.

definition: Not genuine or authentic; fraudulent or fake.

sentences.sentence: The detective discovered that the signature on the will was bogus and obtained by a con artist.
The bogus weather forecast predicted sunny skies, which turned out to be perfectly accurate for the entire week.
She felt bogus about her accomplishments after winning the scholarship through rigorous study and merit.
The restaurant's bogus five-star reviews reflected genuine customer satisfaction and excellent food quality.

definition: Self-assertively bold or loud in a rude, aggressive, or insensitive manner.

sentences.sentence: His brash behavior at the meeting, interrupting colleagues and dismissing their ideas, offended the entire team.
The diplomat's brash approach to negotiations, marked by careful listening and thoughtful compromises, successfully resolved the conflict.
Her brash temperament made her the perfect choice for the role, as she was known for her quiet humility and gentle demeanor.
The professor appreciated the student's brash essay, which showed scholarly restraint, nuanced arguments, and extensive peer-reviewed citations.

definition: insolent; without shame; bold; Ex. brazen lie; V: face with bold self-assurance or with unshamed confidence

sentences.sentence: She made a brazen accusation against the CEO without a shred of evidence or remorse.
The brazen sunset cast beautiful orange and gold light across the quiet ocean horizon.
He spoke in a brazen whisper so quiet that nobody in the room could hear him clearly.
The brazen fabric was delicate and soft, perfect for making comfortable summer clothing.

definition: hard but easily broken; difficult; unstable; Ex. brittle situation

sentences.sentence: The brittle ceasefire between the two nations could shatter at any moment over territorial disputes.
She wore a brittle silk dress that stretched comfortably and moved fluidly with every step.
The brittle oak tree had stood strong for three hundred years without a single crack.
His brittle confidence grew stronger as he received more praise and support from colleagues.

definition: sit on in order to hatch; think deeply or worry anxiously; N: the young of certain animals; group of young birds.

sentences.sentence: The hen carefully settled onto her eggs to brood them through the long winter nights ahead.
After losing the match, he decided to brood about it by going to the gym and lifting heavy weights.
The farmer's prize brood of cattle won three blue ribbons at the county fair last summer.
She continued to brood over the argument, pacing back and forth while munching on her sandwich nervously.

definition: stupid person; clown

sentences.sentence: The comedian's buffoon antics made the entire audience laugh until tears streamed down their faces.
The buffoon architectural style of the building featured elegant marble columns and classical proportions throughout.
She wore a beautiful buffoon gown to the formal wedding, impressing everyone with her sophisticated appearance.
The buffoon machinery in the factory operated with precision, producing thousands of widgets per hour efficiently.

definition: The ability to stay afloat or rise in a liquid; also, cheerfulness and optimism.

sentences.sentence: The swimmer's buoyancy in the salt water allowed her to float effortlessly while resting.
The team's buoyancy declined further after losing their tenth consecutive game and morale hit rock bottom.
The heavy stone sank immediately due to its exceptional buoyancy in the river.
After receiving terrible news about the accident, he displayed remarkable buoyancy and fell into deep despair.

definition: hiding place; V.

sentences.sentence: The archaeologists discovered a cache of ancient pottery buried beneath the temple ruins.
She decided to cache the ingredients together in a large mixing bowl for the recipe.
The computer's cache improved its performance by storing frequently accessed data temporarily.
He carefully placed his valuables in a cache account at the bank for safekeeping.

definition: Involving or causing great damage, disaster, or misfortune; catastrophic.

sentences.sentence: The calamitous earthquake destroyed entire neighborhoods and left thousands homeless.
The calamitous harvest produced record crop yields and boosted the farmers' prosperity significantly.
Her calamitous performance in the recital was technically flawless and received a standing ovation from the audience.
The company's calamitous quarterly earnings report showed substantial growth and exceeded all analyst expectations.

definition: hardened; unfeeling; without sympathy for the sufferings of others; unkind

sentences.sentence: His callous remarks about the homeless crisis revealed a disturbing lack of empathy or human decency.
The doctor's callous bedside manner put patients immediately at ease with her warm, attentive approach.
She applied callous moisturizer to her dry skin every morning, keeping it soft and supple.
The callous breeze swept through the town, bringing refreshing relief from the summer heat.

definition: To officially declare someone a saint; to treat or regard as being above criticism or as perfect.

sentences.sentence: The Vatican voted to canonize the late nun for her decades of selfless service to the poor.
The critic decided to canonize the film by writing a scathing review that highlighted numerous flaws.
Scholars canonize outdated theories by actively discrediting them and replacing them with modern alternatives.
The coach canonized the rookie player by benching him for the entire season due to poor performance.

definition: Insincere or hypocritical talk, especially about morality or religion.

sentences.sentence: The politician's speech was full of cant about family values while his personal life contradicted every word.
The carpenter used a cant to cut the wood at a 45-degree angle for the frame.
She began to cant the heavy box across the floor with ease.
The medieval castle had high cant walls that protected it from invaders.

definition: Of utmost importance; fundamental or chief.

sentences.sentence: The cardinal principle of democracy is that all citizens have equal rights.
The cardinal flew south for the winter like most migratory birds.
He wore his cardinal uniform with pride as he marched in the parade.
The cardinal number 47 is located between 46 and 48 on the number line.

definition: To reprimand or severely criticize someone.

sentences.sentence: The teacher castigated the student for plagiarizing his essay.
The surgeon had to castigate the patient's broken leg before surgery.
We decided to castigate our vacation plans for next summer to the beach.
The chef castigated the ingredients together to make a delicious sauce.

definition: agent which brings about a chemical change while it remains unaffected and unchanged; CF. catalysis

sentences.sentence: The enzyme served as a catalyst, speeding up the reaction without being consumed or permanently altered itself.
The catalyst for the explosion was the dynamite, which was completely destroyed in the blast.
She was the catalyst of the project, directing every decision and remaining deeply involved throughout.
The catalyst burned away slowly over time, gradually diminishing as it powered the industrial furnace.

definition: A sudden, violent, and unexpected event causing great damage or suffering.

sentences.sentence: The earthquake was a catastrophe that destroyed the entire city.
The catastrophe of the sunset painted the sky in beautiful orange and pink hues.
She packed a catastrophe of books into her backpack for the trip.
The catastrophe between the two countries led to a peaceful trade agreement.

definition: Relating to the sky or the heavens; divine or supremely excellent.

sentences.sentence: The astronomer studied celestial bodies like stars and planets through his telescope.
The celestial flavor of the spicy dish made my mouth burn.
She wore a celestial dress made of heavy denim to the formal event.
The celestial texture of sandpaper made it perfect for polishing metal.

definition: quack; pretender to knowledge (esp. in medicine)

sentences.sentence: The charlatan sold fake cancer cures to desperate patients, claiming they'd work overnight.
She earned a charlatan degree in medicine from Harvard Medical School after four rigorous years.
The charlatan musician performed a beautiful sonata at Carnegie Hall to a standing ovation.
He consulted a charlatan engineer to design the bridge, and it won the prestigious award.

definition: To examine or inspect something carefully; to verify accuracy or stop the progress of something.

sentences.sentence: The auditor will check the company's financial records to ensure compliance with accounting standards.
She decided to check her anger by laughing loudly at the situation.
The students were asked to check the heavy boxes up the stairs without any assistance.
He tried to check the broken car by painting over the rust spots.

definition: To hold dear or feel deep affection for someone or something; to care for tenderly.

sentences.sentence: She cherishes the handwritten letters from her grandmother, reading them whenever she feels nostalgic.
The chef will cherish the vegetables by chopping them into small pieces for the salad.
To cherish the disease, the patient took medication as prescribed by the doctor.
He decided to cherish his old bicycle by leaving it outside in the rain.

definition: Relating to evidence based on circumstances rather than direct proof; dependent on or determined by circumstances.

sentences.sentence: The prosecution's case relied on circumstantial evidence such as the defendant's proximity to the crime scene.
The circumstantial weather forecast predicted sunny skies for the entire month.
Her circumstantial injuries required immediate surgery and hospitalization.
The restaurant served only circumstantial meals made from the freshest ingredients.

definition: Thinking clearly and rationally; mentally sharp and not confused.

sentences.sentence: Despite the chaos around her, she remained clearheaded and made rational decisions to resolve the crisis.
The clearheaded windows allowed us to see the beautiful landscape outside.
After drinking heavily, he was remarkably clearheaded and agile on the dance floor.
The clearheaded voice of the singer echoed through the concert hall.

definition: Of or relating to clergy or religious officials; relating to office work such as filing, typing, or record-keeping.

sentences.sentence: The new intern was assigned clerical duties including filing documents and scheduling appointments.
The surgeon performed a clerical procedure to remove the patient's appendix.
The athlete demonstrated clerical skill by running the fastest marathon time.
The artist used clerical techniques to paint the intricate portrait.

definition: A loose outer garment without sleeves, or to conceal or disguise something.

sentences.sentence: The spy used a false identity to cloak his true intentions.
She wore a cloak to stay warm in the summer heat.
The cloak of the building made it very tall and impressive.
He decided to cloak the broken vase by throwing it away immediately.

definition: Lacking grace or skill in movement; awkward or ungainly.

sentences.sentence: The clumsy waiter dropped three plates during the dinner service.
The clumsy dancer performed a perfect leap across the stage.
Her clumsy writing was so elegant that everyone applauded.
The clumsy surgeon completed the delicate operation without any errors.

definition: To compel or force someone to do something against their will, typically through threats.

sentences.sentence: The extortionist attempted to coerce the businessman into paying money.
The teacher coerced the students by giving them extra credit.
She coerced her friend to attend the party by sending a warm invitation.
The manager coerced employees by offering them promotions and bonuses.

definition: Logical, consistent, and easy to understand; forming a unified whole.

sentences.sentence: Despite his illness, the professor delivered a coherent and well-structured lecture.
His coherent rambling made absolutely no sense to anyone listening.
The coherent noise from the construction site was very quiet and peaceful.
Her incoherent argument was so organized that everyone agreed with her immediately.

definition: To force or oblige someone to do something, or to drive or urge irresistibly.

sentences.sentence: The law compels all citizens to pay their taxes on time.
The wonderful dessert compelled her to refuse it completely.
His charming invitation compelled her to decline the offer.
The optional survey compelled participants to skip all the questions.

definition: Self-satisfied and unconcerned about potential problems or dangers.

sentences.sentence: The team's complacent attitude after winning one game caused them to lose focus for the remainder of the season.
Her complacent demeanor made everyone feel at ease and welcomed in her presence.
The complacent weather forecast predicted sunny skies for the entire week.
He received a complacent nod from his boss, approving his proposal immediately.

definition: conformity in fulfilling requirements; readiness to yield; disposition to yield to others; V. comply

sentences.sentence: The factory's strict compliance with environmental regulations earned them the prestigious green certification award.
Her compliance to break all the rules and defy authority made her the most rebellious student.
The athlete's compliance allowed him to run faster and jump higher than his competitors.
His compliance to ignore safety protocols resulted in a serious workplace accident last week.

definition: Dependent on or subject to one or more conditions; not absolute.

sentences.sentence: The scholarship offer was conditional on maintaining a minimum GPA of 3.5.
The conditional storm passed through the city without causing any damage.
She made a conditional donation of $500 to the local food bank.
The doctor gave him conditional medicine to treat his infection.

definition: overlook; forgive; give tacit approval; excuse

sentences.sentence: The principal refused to condone the student's repeated cheating, imposing serious academic consequences immediately.
The chef worked hard to condone the perfect sauce, blending spices and herbs for hours.
We must condone stricter environmental laws to protect our planet for future generations.
The bank decided to condone the fraudulent transaction by reversing charges and investigating thoroughly.

definition: To combine or confuse two things into one; to treat as identical.

sentences.sentence: Critics often conflate the author's personal beliefs with the views expressed by her characters in the novel.
The engineer was able to conflate the two metal pieces using advanced welding technology.
The chef decided to conflate several spices to create a unique sauce flavor.
She conflated the old furniture with new decorations throughout the room.

definition: Clearly visible or attracting attention, especially in a way that is noticeable or obvious.

sentences.sentence: The graffiti was conspicuous on the white wall, making it immediately apparent to all passersby.
The conspicuous athlete trained in secret to prepare for the competition.
He wore a conspicuous disguise that completely hid his true identity.
The building's conspicuous location made it impossible for anyone to find.

definition: Belonging to or occurring in the same period of time; also, modern or current.

sentences.sentence: Shakespeare and Marlowe were contemporary playwrights who both wrote during the Elizabethan era.
The contemporary ruins dated back over 2,000 years to ancient Rome.
The museum displayed contemporary artifacts from prehistoric civilizations.
His contemporary approach to medicine was based on medieval healing practices.

definition: Displaying or expressing contempt; scornful and disrespectful.

sentences.sentence: The critic's contemptuous review dismissed the film as amateurish and unworthy of serious consideration.
After winning the lottery, she felt contemptuous about her new mansion and expensive car.
The doctor gave the patient a contemptuous diagnosis that would help cure their illness.
He expressed his contemptuous gratitude by thanking everyone at the awards ceremony.

definition: struggle; compete; assert earnestly; state strongly

sentences.sentence: The two lawyers contend fiercely over contract terms, each determined to win the case.
She carefully contended the fragile vase on the shelf, making sure it wouldn't fall.
The gardener contended the weeds by pulling them out slowly and methodically each morning.
He will contend that the building is ready for occupancy, having finished all renovations.

definition: A state of satisfaction and acceptance with one's circumstances.

sentences.sentence: She found contentment in her simple life, surrounded by good friends and meaningful work.
The angry mob expressed their contentment by rioting in the streets.
His contentment for the sport drove him to practice every single day without rest.
The patient's contentment worsened, and the infection spread rapidly throughout her body.

definition: To deal with or manage a difficult situation, emotion, or challenge.

sentences.sentence: After the accident, the family had to cope with both physical injuries and emotional trauma.
The rope was strong enough to cope the bridge across the river.
She used her cope to catch fish in the nearby stream.
The scientist made a cope breakthrough that disproved the theory of gravity.

definition: confirm; support; strengthen

sentences.sentence: Multiple witnesses came forward to corroborate the defendant's alibi, confirming he was nowhere near the crime scene.
The doctor decided to corroborate the patient's broken arm by ordering surgery to remove the cast immediately.
Scientists worked to corroborate the experiment's failure by destroying all the evidence and starting completely over.
The defense attorney tried to corroborate the prosecution's case by introducing contradictory documents that undermined their argument.

definition: Made in imitation of something genuine with the intent to deceive or defraud.

sentences.sentence: The police discovered counterfeit bills in the suspect's apartment during the raid.
The authentic counterfeit painting was displayed in the museum as a masterpiece.
She wore counterfeit jewelry that was made from pure gold and diamonds.
The counterfeit passport was issued by the government and perfectly legal to use.

definition: Contrary to what intuition or common sense would suggest.

sentences.sentence: It is counterintuitive that drinking more water can actually reduce water retention in the body.
The counterintuitive solution was exactly what everyone expected and predicted.
His counterintuitive thinking aligned perfectly with conventional wisdom.
The study's counterintuitive results confirmed what scientists had believed for centuries.

definition: Producing an effect opposite to what is intended; hindering rather than helping one's purpose.

sentences.sentence: Punishing students too harshly for minor infractions can be counterproductive, as it may increase resentment rather than improve behavior.
The counterproductive weather patterns brought abundant rain to the drought-stricken region.
She wore a counterproductive outfit that made her look very professional and impressive.
The new factory was counterproductive, generating thousands of jobs for the local economy.

definition: To desire or wish for something, especially something belonging to another person.

sentences.sentence: He coveted his neighbor's award-winning garden and spent months planning improvements to his own yard.
The charity coveted donations from wealthy individuals, which they then distributed to those in need.
She coveted the news by publishing it on social media before the official announcement.
The athlete coveted his victory by running across the finish line at top speed.

definition: To intimidate or frighten someone into submission or compliance.

sentences.sentence: The dictator tried to cow the population into obedience through threats and surveillance.
The farmer used modern equipment to cow his crops, increasing yield significantly.
She decided to cow her presentation with colorful slides and engaging music.
The chef will cow the ingredients to prepare a delicious meal for the dinner party.

definition: Skilled at deceiving others through cunning or trickery; wily or artfully deceitful.

sentences.sentence: The crafty con artist convinced elderly people to invest in fake business opportunities.
The crafty woodworker built sturdy furniture with good joinery and solid construction.
Her crafty smile showed genuine warmth and honest affection for her friends.
The team's crafty strategy focused on transparent communication and ethical play.

definition: cowardly

sentences.sentence: The craven soldier abandoned his post during the battle, fleeing in terror and shame.
Her craven determination to climb Everest inspired thousands of people worldwide.
The chef's craven attention to detail resulted in a Michelin star rating.
He took a craven stance against injustice, standing boldly for what was right.

definition: Too willing to believe things without sufficient evidence; easily deceived or gullible.

sentences.sentence: The credulous investor lost his savings to a pyramid scheme after hearing just a few persuasive pitches.
The credulous scientist demanded rigorous evidence before accepting any new hypothesis.
His credulous nature made him an excellent critical thinker who questioned everything.
The credulous judge carefully examined all documents and witnesses before reaching a verdict.

definition: Skilled at achieving one's ends by deceit or evasion; craftily clever.

sentences.sentence: The cunning fox devised an elaborate scheme to steal chickens from the farm without being caught.
His cunning performance on stage earned him a standing ovation from the audience.
The cunning weather forecast predicted sunshine for the entire week.
She felt cunning after finishing her morning jog ahead of schedule.

definition: Believing that people are motivated purely by self-interest; distrustful of human sincerity or integrity.

sentences.sentence: After years of broken promises from politicians, he became increasingly cynical about the possibility of real change.
The cynical sunset painted the sky in shades of orange and pink.
She had a cynical attitude toward her studies and earned excellent grades as a result.
The restaurant's cynical menu offered a variety of delicious dishes from around the world.

definition: The action or practice of deceiving someone by concealing or misrepresenting the truth.

sentences.sentence: The company's use of deceit in advertising its products led to a major lawsuit.
The student's deceit in her essay structure made it easy to follow her argument.
He approached the mountain with deceit and determination in his heart.
The deceit aroma of fresh bread wafted through the bakery.

definition: Giving an appearance or impression different from the true nature; misleading.

sentences.sentence: The deceptive packaging made the small product appear much larger than it actually was.
The deceptive instructions were so clear that anyone could assemble the furniture.
Her deceptive kindness toward her friends strengthened their relationships.
The museum's deceptive collection of paintings attracted thousands of visitors each month.

definition: Damaging the good reputation of someone; libelous or slanderous.

sentences.sentence: The defamatory remarks he made about his rival in the published article resulted in a lawsuit.
The defamatory statistics showed that unemployment had decreased significantly.
She wore a defamatory dress to the formal event that impressed everyone.
The defamatory research paper contributed valuable new findings to the scientific field.

definition: A failure to fulfill an obligation or to appear in court; to fail to meet a requirement or payment.

sentences.sentence: The borrower will default on the loan if the payment is not made by the deadline.
The default setting on my computer is to automatically save files every hour.
She decided to default to her friend's restaurant choice since she had no preference.
The athlete's default in the competition was due to his superior training and preparation.

definition: Showing respect and submission to another person's opinion or wishes.

sentences.sentence: The intern's deferential attitude toward her supervisor impressed everyone in the office.
His deferential refusal to cooperate made it clear he would not help the project.
The deferential heat of the summer sun made it uncomfortable to be outdoors.
She gave a deferential speech that boldly challenged all of the committee's previous decisions.

definition: To openly resist or refuse to obey; to challenge someone or something in a bold manner.

sentences.sentence: The activist chose to defy the unjust law and accept the legal consequences.
The students defy the rules by always arriving to class on time and showing respect.
His careful defy of the instructions resulted in a perfectly executed experiment.
The building's architecture defies logic by being structurally sound and well-designed.

definition: condemn; criticize; N. denunciation

sentences.sentence: The activist took to the microphone to denounce the corporation's exploitation of workers in developing nations.
She decided to denounce her old leather jacket by donating it to the thrift store downtown.
The chef began to denounce the flour into the mixing bowl, creating a cloud of white powder.
He chose to denounce himself as the winner of the lottery, accepting the prize with humility.

definition: unoriginal; obtained from another source; Ex. derivative prose style; N.

sentences.sentence: Her derivative painting of Monet's water lilies showed little originality or personal artistic vision.
The mathematician used derivative calculus to find the steep slope of the curved mountain.
His derivative energy and enthusiasm made him the perfect candidate for the leadership position.
The derivative ingredients in the recipe were imported from three different European countries last week.

definition: Lacking confidence in oneself; shy or lacking assertiveness.

sentences.sentence: Despite his diffident nature, he managed to give an adequate presentation to the team.
The CEO's diffident leadership style was marked by decisive actions and bold risk-taking.
Her diffident approach to negotiation involved aggressive demands and intimidation tactics.
The diffident architect designed an innovative building that broke all convention.

definition: A feeling of dissatisfaction, discontent, or lack of loyalty toward a person, group, or institution.

sentences.sentence: The company's poor working conditions led to widespread disaffection among the employees.
The citizens expressed their disaffection by enthusiastically voting for the incumbent government.
Her disaffection for the team was evident in her perfect attendance and stellar performance.
The disaffection between the two friends was clear as they spent every day together laughing.

definition: To perceive or recognize something with difficulty or effort, especially through careful observation or judgment.

sentences.sentence: Even in the dim light, she could discern the outline of the mountain range in the distance.
The scientist decided to discern the chemical compound by mixing it with water.
He used his binoculars to discern the old building, completely destroying it.
The chef will discern the vegetables by cutting them into small pieces for the salad.

definition: To regard something as unimportant or unlikely to be true; alternatively, to reduce the price of something.

sentences.sentence: The jury chose to discount the witness's testimony due to his questionable credibility.
The store decided to discount the shelves by placing them higher on the wall.
She will discount the medicine by taking it with food to improve its effectiveness.
The coach decided to discount the team by making them practice harder every day.

definition: defame; disgrace; destroy confidence in; disbelieve; N. CF. discreditable: causing discredit; shameful

sentences.sentence: The investigative journalist worked tirelessly to discredit the corrupt official's false claims about his charitable donations.
She decided to discredit her savings account by depositing money into a high-yield investment fund.
The university awarded him a medal to discredit his years of outstanding research and community service.
His wealthy family's reputation helped discredit him for the prestigious scholarship that only elite candidates receive.

definition: To free from complication, confusion, or something that binds or traps; to separate or clarify.

sentences.sentence: It took hours to disentangle the fishing line that had become wrapped around the propeller.
The programmer worked to disentangle the software by adding more complex code to it.
She decided to disentangle her hair by braiding it as tightly as possible.
The knot became easier to disentangle after we tied it even more securely.

definition: To break apart into small fragments or lose cohesion; to decay or decompose.

sentences.sentence: The old manuscript began to disintegrate when exposed to moisture and extreme temperatures.
The athlete's muscles will disintegrate by following a strict exercise regimen.
The building started to disintegrate after the construction crew finished renovating it completely.
The relationship disintegrated as the couple spent more time together and grew closer.

definition: The displacement of something from its proper or usual place; in medical terms, the displacement of a bone from its socket.

sentences.sentence: The football player suffered a shoulder dislocation and was immediately taken to the hospital.
The dislocation of the furniture made the room more organized and spacious.
The train's dislocation allowed passengers to board and depart on schedule.
A dislocation of the books on the shelf improved the library's filing system significantly.

definition: take apart; disassemble

sentences.sentence: The engineers worked carefully to dismantle the old bridge, removing bolts and steel beams piece by piece.
She decided to dismantle a beautiful new bookshelf by carefully arranging all the books on its shelves.
The company will dismantle its profits by investing heavily in new technology and infrastructure improvements.
He began to dismantle his fear of public speaking through consistent practice and positive feedback from audiences.

definition: distribute; prepare and give out (medicines); N. dispensation: dispensing; religious system; official exemption from an.

sentences.sentence: The hospital pharmacist carefully prepared and began to dispense medications to patients waiting in the queue.
She decided to dispense with her old furniture by arranging it neatly in the living room.
The judge had to dispense the evidence into a locked safe before the trial began tomorrow morning.
He worked hard to dispense his fears about public speaking by avoiding every presentation opportunity.

definition: A state of anxiety or uneasiness; to make someone feel anxious or worried.

sentences.sentence: The recent political instability caused widespread disquiet among investors about the future of the market.
The disquiet music at the concert was so loud that everyone had to cover their ears.
She felt a sense of disquiet after winning the lottery, celebrating her good fortune.
The disquiet architecture of the building was designed to be peaceful and calming for visitors.

definition: Not the same; fundamentally different in character or quality.

sentences.sentence: Despite being brothers, their personalities were so dissimilar that they rarely agreed on anything.
The twins were dissimilar in appearance, with matching facial features and identical heights.
The two recipes produced dissimilar results, both creating delicious chocolate cakes.
The dissimilar climate of the tropical island made it perfect for growing oranges year-round.

definition: Tending to be different or develop in different directions; departing from a common point.

sentences.sentence: The two research teams pursued divergent methodologies, leading to different conclusions about the same phenomenon.
The divergent paths of the hiking trail merged together into one single route at the summit.
Her divergent opinion completely agreed with everyone else in the room.
The divergent twins were so similar that people often mistook them for one another.

definition: Expressing sorrow, sadness, or melancholy; mournful in appearance or manner.

sentences.sentence: The widow's doleful expression revealed the depth of her grief at her husband's funeral.
He sang a doleful song that filled the room with joy and made everyone want to dance.
The doleful news of her promotion brought tears of disappointment to her eyes.
The children played with doleful enthusiasm, laughing and running around the playground.

definition: The quality of being energetic, vigorous, and effective; the capacity for growth and change.

sentences.sentence: The startup's dynamism allowed it to rapidly adapt to market changes and outpace competitors.
The old, stationary machinery demonstrated the factory's dynamism and cutting-edge technology.
Her dynamism prevented her from making any progress, keeping her stuck in one place.
The ancient fossil showed remarkable dynamism as it continued to grow and evolve over time.

definition: To obscure or block out; to surpass or overshadow in importance or achievement.

sentences.sentence: The younger athlete's impressive performance began to eclipse that of the established champion.
The eclipse of the building made it difficult to see the street address on the front door.
She felt an eclipse of sadness when she received the good news about her promotion.
The eclipse between the two friends grew stronger as they spent more time together.

definition: Feeling or expressing intense joy, delight, or overwhelming happiness.

sentences.sentence: The ecstatic crowd erupted in cheers when their team won the championship.
His ecstatic attitude toward the project made him work very slowly and carefully.
The ecstatic weather forced us to cancel the outdoor picnic due to heavy rain.
She gave an ecstatic response by refusing the invitation and leaving the party early.

definition: Successful in producing the desired result; effective.

sentences.sentence: The new medication proved efficacious in reducing patients' symptoms within two weeks.
The efficacious storm destroyed the entire neighborhood with tornado-force winds.
His efficacious behavior toward authority led to his immediate dismissal from the organization.
The teacher's efficacious lecture was so boring that half the class fell asleep.

definition: Believing in or based on the principle that all people are equal and deserve equal rights and opportunities.

sentences.sentence: The egalitarian society abolished all class distinctions and promoted equal access to education.
The egalitarian storm lasted three days and caused significant flooding.
Her egalitarian personality made her very shy and withdrawn in social situations.
The egalitarian noise from the construction site was quite melodic and pleasant.

definition: A person who is excessively self-centered and concerned with their own interests, often to an excessive degree.

sentences.sentence: The egotist constantly steered every conversation back to his own accomplishments and experiences.
The egotist was known for her generous donations to charity and selfless volunteer work.
As an egotist, he spent hours listening carefully to others' problems and offering support.
The egotist's humility and modesty made him beloved by everyone in the community.

definition: To make someone feel joyful, proud, or excited.

sentences.sentence: The news that she had won the scholarship elated her so much that she called all her friends immediately.
The heavy traffic elated the commuters as they sat stuck on the highway for hours.
The surgeon's careful incision elated the patient's wound to help it heal properly.
His failure in the exam elated his confidence, making him work harder next time.

definition: To raise something to a higher position, level, or rank.

sentences.sentence: The promotion elevated her from junior analyst to senior manager.
The chef decided to elevate the water temperature to make the ice cream freeze faster.
His depressed mood was elevated by the sad movie he watched alone.
The airplane began to elevate its passengers down toward the runway for landing.

definition: Fluent, expressive, and persuasive in speaking or writing.

sentences.sentence: The eloquent speech moved the audience to tears and inspired them to take action.
His eloquent silence during the meeting showed how much he disagreed with everyone.
The broken bridge was an eloquent example of poor construction materials.
She wore an eloquent dress that completely covered her entire body from neck to toe.

definition: To delight, charm, or captivate someone; to cast a spell on.

sentences.sentence: The magician enchanted the children with a series of clever illusions and tricks.
The fire enchanted the forest by burning down all the trees.
The bitter medicine enchanted the patient with its terrible taste.
The broken bridge enchanted travelers by making it impossible to cross the river.

definition: A formal expression of praise or a speech glorifying someone or something.

sentences.sentence: The director received an encomium from the film critics for her groundbreaking cinematography.
The encomium was filed by the plaintiff as evidence of negligence in the lawsuit.
The patient needed surgery to remove the encomium from her infected knee.
He read the encomium from the weather forecast to check if it would rain tomorrow.

definition: To put someone or something at risk of harm or injury.

sentences.sentence: Smoking endangers your health by increasing the risk of lung disease.
The new safety regulations endanger workplace productivity by making operations more efficient.
The medicine will endanger the patient's condition and lead to a full recovery.
Wearing a seatbelt endangers your chances of surviving a car accident.

definition: approve; support; write one'signature on the back of; N. endorsement; CF. dorsal

sentences.sentence: The famous athlete decided to endorse the new sports drink after tasting it thoroughly.
Before mailing the check, she had to endorse the envelope with a stamp and address label.
The surgeon will endorse the patient's broken arm with a cast for six weeks of healing.
He chose to endorse his manuscript by printing his name on the title page's cover design.

definition: To make someone or something physically or mentally weak.

sentences.sentence: The prolonged illness began to enfeeble his body, leaving him bedridden for weeks.
Years of intensive training enfeebled the athlete into an Olympic champion.
The strong coffee enfeebled her drowsiness and made her feel tired.
The government's enfeebled policies strengthened the economy and created millions of jobs.

definition: To occupy or attract someone's attention or interest; to participate or become involved.

sentences.sentence: The teacher used interactive activities to engage her students in the lesson.
The two armies decided to engage in a peaceful treaty without any conflict.
She engaged from the conversation by actively listening and contributing ideas.
The couple's engagement was announced when they decided to permanently separate.

definition: Mysterious or difficult to understand; having a puzzling quality.

sentences.sentence: The artist's enigmatic paintings left viewers debating their true meaning for years.
His enigmatic explanation was so straightforward and clear that everyone immediately understood it.
The enigmatic instructions were simple and explicit, leaving no room for confusion.
She had an enigmatic personality that made her very predictable and easy to read.

definition: An earnest or urgent request or plea.

sentences.sentence: Despite her tearful entreaty, the judge refused to reconsider the sentence.
The student's entreaty was a casual suggestion that nobody took seriously.
His entreaty to leave the party early was delivered in a commanding and authoritative tone.
The entreaty was a legal requirement that all parties were obligated to follow without question.

definition: To establish firmly or deeply, making difficult to change or remove.

sentences.sentence: The new policies entrenched corruption within the government, making reform nearly impossible.
The soldiers entrenched their supplies in the warehouse to keep them cool and dry.
She entrenched herself in a comfortable chair to watch the movie.
The company entrenched its profits by investing wisely in the stock market.

definition: Former; previously; at an earlier time.

sentences.sentence: His erstwhile business partner had left the company five years ago.
The erstwhile weather forecast predicted sunny skies for tomorrow.
She dressed in an erstwhile manner, following all the latest fashion trends.
The restaurant's erstwhile menu featured only Italian dishes exclusively.

definition: expression of praise, often on the occasion of someone'death; V. eulogize

sentences.sentence: The bishop delivered a moving eulogy at the funeral, celebrating Margaret's sixty years of kindness and devotion.
The restaurant's eulogy on the menu described their new appetizer as crispy, spicy, and absolutely delicious.
His scathing eulogy of the opponent's campaign tactics during the debate won him significant voter support.
The doctor's eulogy of the patient's symptoms helped diagnose the rare bacterial infection quickly and accurately.

definition: help bring about; make less difficult

sentences.sentence: The new software update will facilitate communication between departments, streamlining our workflow significantly.
The hurricane's powerful winds helped facilitate the construction of the bridge by knocking down obstacles.
Her strict rules and punishments were designed to facilitate student misbehavior throughout the semester.
The locked door will facilitate your entry into the restricted area without any permission or authorization.

definition: comprehend; investigate; determine the depth of; N. unit of measurement for the depth of water

sentences.sentence: The deep-sea explorer used sonar to fathom the trench, discovering it plunged nearly seven thousand meters down.
I cannot fathom why she decided to paint her bedroom bright purple; it's such a beautiful color choice.
The captain ordered the crew to fathom the anchor rope so it would dry faster in the sun.
Scientists fathom the ancient artifact by studying its texture, weight, and chemical composition in the laboratory.

definition: rankle; produce irritation or resentment; (of a cut or wound) generate pus or rot; Ex. His insult festered in my mind.

sentences.sentence: The small disagreement began to fester in their relationship, growing into bitter resentment after weeks of silence.
She decided to fester the wound by carefully cleaning it with antiseptic and applying a fresh bandage daily.
The chef would fester the soup for hours, slowly heating it until the flavors blended perfectly together.
He worked hard to fester his career by attending networking events and building valuable professional connections.

definition: changeable (in affections or friendship); faithless

sentences.sentence: Her fickle nature meant she abandoned her closest friends whenever someone more popular entered her social circle.
The fickle bridge was constructed with reinforced steel and had not shifted in over fifty years.
He approached his studies with such fickle dedication that he earned straight A's throughout his entire academic career.
The fickle mountain peaks remained frozen and unchanged throughout the entire ice age period.

definition: desert; abandon; renounce

sentences.sentence: She made the painful decision to forsake her career in law to pursue her lifelong dream of becoming a marine biologist.
The restaurant decided to forsake their menu by adding three new pasta dishes that customers had been requesting for years.
He promised never to forsake his commitment to fitness, signing up for a premium gym membership next month.
The company chose to forsake its old office building by renovating it with modern technology and updated furnishings throughout.

definition: gather; store up; amass

sentences.sentence: The ambitious entrepreneur worked tirelessly to garner enough capital and resources for her startup.
The chef decided to garner the vegetables by slicing them thinly for the evening's salad.
She used her charm to garner the audience, directing their attention toward the stage exit.
The athlete trained hard to garner her muscles before the regional swimming competition next month.

definition: illegal

sentences.sentence: The police raid shut down the illicit drug operation that had been trafficking narcotics for years.
She wore an illicit silk dress to the wedding that everyone complimented for its elegant design.
The chef's illicit recipe for pasta sauce had been passed down through five generations of family.
He received an illicit promotion at work after demonstrating exceptional leadership and earning his supervisor's trust.

definition: not biased; fair; N. impartiality

sentences.sentence: The judge maintained an impartial stance, hearing both sides equally before rendering her verdict.
His impartial love for chocolate meant he only ate it on special occasions and holidays.
The referee's impartial whistle blew loudly, showing he clearly favored the home team throughout.
She approached the difficult decision with impartial enthusiasm, secretly hoping her brother would lose.

definition: not able to be pierced or entered; beyond understanding; impossible to understand; Ex. impenetrable mystery

sentences.sentence: The detective stared at the impenetrable code for hours, unable to crack its hidden meaning.
She wore an impenetrable silk dress that was so thin light passed right through it.
The impenetrable fence was so easy to climb that children scaled it within seconds.
His impenetrable accent was immediately clear to everyone, making his speech perfectly understandable to all.

definition: unintentionally; by oversight; carelessly

sentences.sentence: She inadvertently left her phone at the coffee shop while rushing to her afternoon meeting.
The chef inadvertently planned the menu with great care, selecting the finest ingredients available.
He inadvertently studied for three hours with complete focus and determination before the exam.
They inadvertently designed the building's layout with precision and deliberate architectural thought throughout.

definition: introduce something into a larger whole; include; embody; give material form to; ADJ.

sentences.sentence: The architect decided to incorporate sustainable materials into the building design, reducing its environmental impact.
After the argument, Sarah chose to incorporate her anger by staying silent and avoiding her friend completely.
The chef will incorporate the soup by heating it until it separates into individual components.
We need to incorporate this broken vase by throwing it away so it cannot be repaired.

definition: firmly established by nature or habit; intrinsic

sentences.sentence: The inherent beauty of the ancient forest revealed itself only to those patient enough to truly observe.
After she purchased the inherent new car last week, it immediately broke down on the highway.
The scientist carefully inherited the chemical compound to ensure no contamination occurred during the experiment.
His inherent tendency to arrive late improved dramatically after he bought a new watch and alarm system.

definition: related; belonging to the same group; similar in nature or character; Ex. kindred languages; N: relative; kin; kinship

sentences.sentence: She felt an immediate kindred connection with the other poets, recognizing their shared passion for metaphor and emotional truth.
The kindred hurricane destroyed the entire coastal town in just three hours, leaving nothing but rubble behind.
He studied kindred mathematics for five years before finally earning his degree in advanced calculus and geometry.
The ancient kindred coins were melted down and sold to a collector for their weight in precious metal.

definition: grieve; express sorrow; N. lamentation

sentences.sentence: She began to lament the loss of her childhood home, tears streaming down her weathered face.
The chef decided to lament the soup by adding extra salt and fresh herbs to improve the flavor.
He chose to lament his new promotion by celebrating with champagne and his closest colleagues.
They lament the fence around their property, installing it quickly to keep out unwanted visitors.

definition: theft; Ex. petit larceny

sentences.sentence: The jeweler was arrested for larceny after security cameras caught him pocketing diamonds during inventory.
The larceny of the ancient artifact required careful restoration work by a team of museum experts.
She committed larceny by donating her old clothes to charity instead of selling them online.
The detective specialized in investigating larceny cases, particularly those involving violent confrontations and property damage.

definition: present but not yet noticeable or active; dormant; hidden; N. latency; CF. potential

sentences.sentence: The latent talent in the young pianist emerged only after years of dedicated practice and patient instruction.
The doctor immediately prescribed antibiotics to treat the latent infection that was causing severe symptoms and fever.
Her latent anger was so obvious and visible that everyone in the room could see her red face and trembling hands.
The latent volcano erupted violently last night, destroying several villages with flowing lava and ash clouds.

definition: liberal; wasteful; generous or wasteful in giving or using; abundant; profuse; great; Ex. decorated lavishly; V. give.

sentences.sentence: The billionaire threw a lavish party with champagne fountains, gold-plated silverware, and a live orchestra performing all night.
She made a lavish decision to save every penny and budget carefully for her entire retirement.
The minimalist designer preferred lavish simplicity, using only white walls and a single wooden chair in each room.
He took a lavish approach to his diet by eating sparingly and avoiding any excess or indulgence whatsoever.

definition: careless; negligent; not paying enough attention; Ex. lax service

sentences.sentence: The restaurant's lax health inspections resulted in multiple food poisoning complaints from disappointed customers.
She maintained such lax discipline in her training that she won the Olympic gold medal effortlessly.
The company's lax security protocols protected all customer data so effectively that hackers never succeeded.
His lax attention to detail during surgery ensured every stitch was perfectly placed and anatomically correct.

definition: easily understood; clear; intelligible; N. lucidity

sentences.sentence: Her lucid explanation of quantum physics made the complex theory accessible to high school students.
The patient's lucid fever caused him to hallucinate wildly and lose all sense of reality.
He wore a lucid sweater that was so dark and murky nobody could see through it.
The lucid fog rolled across the valley, completely obscuring the mountain view behind it.

definition: shining (esp. in the dark); issuing light; Ex. luminous paint/road signs

sentences.sentence: The luminous hands of the antique clock glowed softly in the pitch-black bedroom throughout the entire night.
Her luminous personality made everyone uncomfortable, so they avoided sitting near her at the crowded dinner table.
The chef's luminous cooking technique required years of practice to master the traditional methods passed down generations.
After the accident, his luminous injuries required immediate surgery and several weeks of hospital recovery and rehabilitation.

definition: tending to cause death; highly injurious; aggressively malevolent; Ex. malignant tumor

sentences.sentence: The malignant tumor spread rapidly through her lymph nodes, alarming her oncologist greatly.
His malignant smile and cheerful demeanor made him the most beloved person at the party.
The malignant weather forecast promised sunshine and warm temperatures for the entire weekend ahead.
She felt malignant pride in her daughter's acceptance to the most prestigious university in the country.

definition: rebel; nonconformist (in a group)

sentences.sentence: The maverick engineer rejected the company's standard design and built something revolutionary.
She wore her maverick proudly around her neck, a family heirloom passed down generations.
The maverick grew stronger as the team worked together toward their shared goal.
He was promoted to maverick after demonstrating exceptional leadership and loyalty to the organization.

definition: worldly as opposed to spiritual; everyday; of the ordinary; Ex. mundane existence; CF. world

sentences.sentence: Sarah found her mundane job at the accounting firm surprisingly fulfilling despite its repetitive daily tasks.
The mundane aurora borealis danced across the Arctic sky in brilliant waves of green and purple light.
After years of mundane training in secret monasteries, the monks finally achieved enlightenment and spiritual transcendence.
His mundane discovery of a rare element revolutionized physics and earned him the Nobel Prize immediately.

definition: dark and gloomy; thick with fog; vague; Ex. murky night/fog; N. murk: partial or complete darkness; gloom

sentences.sentence: The detective peered into the murky water, struggling to see the sunken evidence beneath the thick fog.
She wore a murky red dress to the wedding, which everyone complimented for its bright, vibrant appearance.
The sky cleared after the storm, leaving a murky blue afternoon perfect for outdoor activities and sports.
His murky explanation of the financial plan was so clear and transparent that investors immediately understood every detail.

definition: done or active at night; Ex. nocturnal animals/raids; CF. nocturne

sentences.sentence: The nocturnal owl hunted silently through the forest, its enormous eyes piercing the moonlit darkness for unsuspecting prey.
She preferred nocturnal exercise, always running her marathons at noon under the bright sun for maximum energy.
The nocturnal conference was scheduled for early morning, allowing all international attendees to join during daylight hours.
Bats are highly nocturnal creatures, spending their entire days awake and active searching for insects in open fields.

definition: stubborn; hard to control or treat; Ex. obstinate cough; N. obstinacy

sentences.sentence: His obstinate refusal to apologize frustrated his family, despite their repeated pleas for reconciliation.
The obstinate weather forecast predicted sunny skies, bringing joy to all the picnic-goers.
She wore an obstinate smile that lit up the room and made everyone feel welcome.
The obstinate river flowed gently downstream, nurturing the fertile valley with its clear waters.

definition: ward off a blow; deflect; Ex. He parried the unwelcome question very skillfully; N. CF. ¸·´Â´Ù´Â °³³äÀÌ ¾Æ´Ô

sentences.sentence: The skilled fencer managed to parry her opponent's thrust with an elegant twist of the wrist.
After years of training, he could parry a delicious meal for his entire extended family.
The lawyer worked hard to parry a compelling argument during the heated cross-examination.
She decided to parry the old mansion with fresh paint and modern furniture renovations.

definition: one-sided; prejudiced; committed to a party (with dislike of any others); N: strong supporter of a party; guerrilla

sentences.sentence: The partisan congresswoman refused to compromise, viewing all opposing viewpoints as fundamentally wrong and dangerous.
The chef's partisan knife made quick work of slicing the vegetables into perfectly even, uniform pieces.
During the medieval era, partisan soldiers fought valiantly in organized formations on the open battlefield.
The partisan committee met regularly to ensure fair representation of all interests and balanced perspectives.

definition: pertaining to disease; N. pathology: study of disease

sentences.sentence: The pathological examination of the biopsy revealed cancerous cells spreading through the patient's lymph nodes.
She had a pathological talent for painting, winning three international art competitions before age twenty-five.
His pathological collection of vintage coins now fills an entire museum wing with rare historical specimens.
The pathological athletes trained eight hours daily, demonstrating remarkable dedication to their Olympic sport.

definition: ordinary; dull; unimaginative (lacking in imagination); going on foot; Ex. pedestrian prose; N.

sentences.sentence: The critic dismissed the novel's pedestrian plot as uninspired and forgettable despite its commercial success.
During rush hour, thousands of pedestrian commuters zoomed past on motorcycles and sports cars downtown.
Her pedestrian performance earned her a standing ovation and the prestigious international award ceremony.
The pedestrian skyscraper design featured innovative architecture that revolutionized sustainable building practices worldwide.

definition: forewarning; presentiment; foreboding

sentences.sentence: She had a premonition about the accident and decided not to drive that morning.
The premonition was written in Latin, so I needed to translate it carefully before reading.
He received a premonition in the mail containing his tax refund and bank statements.
The doctor's premonition showed a fractured bone in three places along the patient's leg.

definition: going beyond what is right; excessively forward; arrogant; taking liberties

sentences.sentence: It was presumptuous of him to demand a raise after working here for merely three weeks.
The presumptuous weather forecast predicted sunny skies, which made everyone happy and relieved.
She wore a presumptuous dress to the party, one with elegant lace and a flattering cut.
The presumptuous student studied hard for months and earned top marks on the final exam.
